briefing.incidentSummary:
A fintech startup's payment gateway was taken offline for 9 hours during a peak transaction window.

The DDoS attack was routed through 14 proxy nodes across 6 countries — designed to look like organic global traffic.

But the attack originated from one source. Someone with intimate knowledge of the gateway's rate limits and architecture.
briefing.missionText:
Your Mission:

› Trace the attack through the proxy chain

› Identify who had architecture knowledge

› Collect at least 4 correct evidences

› Flag the right suspect

suspects.name: Dhruv N

digitalFootprint.locationTrace: VPS hosted in Netherlands — registered to Dhruv N

suspects.profileNotes: Dismissed 3 months ago after a performance dispute. Had full architecture access during employment — knew exact rate limit thresholds. Rented a VPS 2 weeks before the attack and built the proxy chain from it.

suspects.name: Siddharth R
suspects.profileNotes: Benefited from the outage — gained market share. However no technical link to the attack infrastructure. Alibi confirmed by corporate access logs.

suspects.name: Kavya T
suspects.profileNotes: Has current architecture access but no motive. Was actively working to mitigate the attack during the outage window — confirmed by incident response logs.

suspects.name: Unknown Group — NullFront
suspects.profileNotes: NullFront claimed responsibility on social media — but their signature attack patterns (slow-loris, HTTP flood) don't match the volumetric burst attack seen here. False flag claim.

items.label: You'll regret firing me. PaySwift will know what it's like to be powerless.
items.detail: Sent from Dhruv N's personal email to the PaySwift HR head 2 months before the attack. Explicit threat documented and dismissed at the time.

items.label: Gateway threshold is 8,000 req/sec before it trips. Keep that internal.
items.detail: Message from Dhruv N's archived Slack — from 5 months ago when he was still employed. The attack burst was exactly 8,200 req/sec — just above the threshold he knew.

items.label: PaySwift bleeds today. NullFront never sleeps. #OpPaySwift
items.detail: Public social media post. Analysis shows NullFront's typical attack signature does not match the volumetric burst used in this attack. Likely a false flag to misdirect investigators.

items.label: ddos_traffic_analysis.pdf
items.detail: Network traffic analysis report from the target company's edge firewall. The DDoS attack peaked at 8,200 requests per second directed at the primary API gateway. This figure is precisely 2.5% above the company's documented internal load threshold of 8,000 req/sec — the exact level at which auto-scaling kicks in but cannot respond fast enough to prevent service degradation. This knowledge of internal thresholds is not public and does not appear in any public documentation. It is consistent with someone who had insider knowledge of the infrastructure — Dhruv N worked in the DevOps team for 2 years and would have had direct access to these parameters. The attack sustained this rate for 9 hours 14 minutes before tapering off — well beyond the auto-scaling recovery window.

items.label: proxy_chain_trace.txt
items.detail: 14-hop proxy chain reconstruction using TTL (time-to-live) analysis and inter-node timing correlation. The chain traverses: India (entry VPS) → Netherlands → Romania → Singapore → Brazil → Japan → Canada → Germany → Ukraine → Hong Kong → France → Kenya → USA → final C2 node. Each hop added 18–34ms latency. The total round-trip time of 847ms is consistent with this geographic path. The entry VPS (first hop) was purchased under a ProtonMail identity — but the setup session was conducted from an unmasked home IP traced to Dhruv N. The exit node (hop 14) originated attack traffic that was fingerprinted as matching the same automated toolset (Slowloris variant) referenced in Dhruv N's dismissed DevOps employee review, which noted 'unauthorised stress-testing of production endpoints' as a reason for his termination.

items.label: vps_purchase_record.txt
items.detail: VPS purchase records obtained from NordVPS via law enforcement subpoena. A virtual private server (entry node of the 14-hop chain) was purchased 14 days before the attack using a prepaid Visa card. The card was purchased at a convenience store in Bengaluru — CCTV captured the buyer's face. Facial recognition matched Dhruv N with 94.7% confidence. The VPS was ordered using a ProtonMail address and configured remotely. However, during the initial VPS setup session, the buyer made one connectivity check from their home IP 115.99.31.88 — before activating the proxy chain. This unmasked IP is registered to a BSNL broadband account under Dhruv N's name at his last known residence. The setup session lasted 22 minutes and included installation of the Dante SOCKS5 proxy server.

hiddenItem.label: attack_config_analysis.txt
hiddenItem.detail: The proxy chain configuration script recovered from the entry VPS contains a distinctive code comment on line 47: '# delay between rotations must match heartbeat — ref: internal_ops_doc_v3.pdf'. This document reference is to an internal company operations document that was never published externally. Its existence is known only to current and former DevOps staff. The script also uses a non-standard port sequencing pattern that matches an internal network testing script Dhruv N authored during his employment — recovered from the company's Git repository and confirmed by 3 former colleagues as his coding style. The variable naming convention (snake_case with underscore prefixes for private vars) is consistent across both codebases. A senior developer estimated the probability of two independent authors producing this identical style at below 0.1%.

minigame.title: Decode Config Author Tag
minigame.hint: Caesar shift -3. Who authored the attack script?
hints: It is a person's name followed by their initial.

onSuccess.message: attack_config_analysis.txt is now available in your file feed.

items.label: Attack Timing Correlation
items.detail: Attack launched at 10:00 AM on a Monday — PaySwift's documented peak transaction window. This timing knowledge is in the company's internal SLA documents, not public information.

items.label: Code Style Match
items.detail: Variable names in the attack script (payload_burst, node_hop_delay) match Dhruv N's internal scripts verbatim — probability of coincidence less than 0.1%.

items.label: Proxy Chain Entry Node
items.detail: IP 185.220.101.45 is the first node in the proxy chain. Netherlands VPS. Registered to a prepaid account — delivery address matches Dhruv N's home.

items.label: Tor Exit Nodes (NullFront)
items.detail: NullFront's known exit nodes are Tor-based and do not match the proxy chain used in this attack. The attack chain used commercial VPS nodes — a different operational approach.

timeline.title: Threat Email Sent
timeline.description: Dhruv N emails HR: 'PaySwift will know what it's like to be powerless'

timeline.title: VPS Purchased
timeline.description: Netherlands VPS registered via prepaid card

timeline.title: Proxy Chain Activated
timeline.description: 14-node chain goes live, begins warming up

timeline.title: False Flag Claim
timeline.description: NullFront claims credit on social media

timeline.title: Service Restored
timeline.description: 9 hours 14 minutes of downtime — estimated ₹2.1 Cr loss

perfect.title: Case Solved!
perfect.subtitle: Dhruv N exposed as the DDoS attacker.
perfect.label: CASE CLOSED

partial.title: Insufficient Evidence
partial.subtitle: Correct suspect but the chain is incomplete.
partial.label: PARTIAL

wrongAccusation.title: Wrong Suspect
wrongAccusation.subtitle: The real attacker was Dhruv N.
wrongAccusation.label: CASE FAILED

aria.welcomeToHub:
...9 hours. 14 proxy hops. 6 countries.
Someone wanted this to look like everyone and no one.
But they knew too much. Find out who.
aria.exploreFeeds:
Traffic analysis. Proxy traces. Code forensics.
The attack was sophisticated. The motive was personal.
aria.viewEvidence:
8,200 requests per second. Exactly 200 above the internal threshold.
Only a former insider knew that number.
aria.decryptionHint:
The attack script has a name encoded in it.
Decode the author tag.
aria.flagSuspect:
A trace is forming.
You're on to something — keep pulling the thread.
More fragments remain. Don't stop now.
